key_insight: Tuning the oxygen/fuel ratio in HVOF spraying strongly changes the microstructure and performance of equiatomic CoCrFeNiMn high-entropy alloy coatings, with one regime delivering the best hardness and wear resistance.
engineering_relevance: This work shows that CoCrFeNiMn HVOF coatings can be engineered as durable protective layers for severe wear environments, and that thermodynamic prediction can help guide process selection.
method: Gas-atomized CoCrFeNiMn powder was deposited by HVOF using kerosene fuel at different oxygen/fuel ratios.
Phase and microstructural changes were characterized with XRD, SEM, and EDS.
Mechanical and tribological behavior was evaluated through microhardness, elastic modulus, and wear testing.
evidence_signal: The best HVOF regime achieved 783.8 HV microhardness and a wear rate of 7.45 × 10^-5 mm3 × N^-1 × m^-1, matching trends predicted by Scheil-based thermodynamic modeling.
watch_item: Process sensitivity is high: coating properties depend strongly on HVOF parameter control, so results may not transfer directly without careful optimization and scale-up validation.
paper_title: Microstructure and Mechanical Properties of Equiatomic CoCrFeNiMn High-Entropy Alloy Coatings Fabricated by High-Velocity Oxygen Fuel Spraying
